summary: Graham argues that object-oriented programming is a useful technique in some situations but shouldn't dominate how all programs are written. He claims most reasons for its popularity are weak or context-bound, and prefers composing specific OO features as needed rather than committing to full OO design.
key_points: OO is a tool, not a universal paradigm; forcing everything into classes is unnecessary.
OO often compensates for limitations in languages lacking closures or macros.
Big-company software processes favor OO because it constrains large teams, even at the cost of bloat and duplication.
OO can create the appearance of productivity via extensive scaffolding and ceremony.
Overloading and other OO sub-concepts can be used without committing to full OO systems.
money_quotes: My own feeling is that object-oriented programming is a useful technique in some cases, but it isn't something that has to pervade every program you write.
Object-oriented programming is popular in big companies, because it suits the way they write software.
Object-oriented programming generates a lot of what looks like work.
Overloading, for example, is not intrinsically tied to classes.
But it seems more dangerous to put stuff in that you've never needed because it's thought to be a good idea.
key_insight: Treat object-oriented programming as one of several tools and adopt its specific features only when they genuinely fit the problem.
